fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, filing this here so we don't lose track. We need a tool that auto-generates those boilerplate constants files for our Java/Kotlin projects at build time. Right now devs are hand-writing these things and they keep going stale or getting out of sync between prod and test code. Super painful.

Basically someone describes what constants they want (name, type, value) somewhere in the build config, and the tool spits out a ready-to-compile source file in the right place. Should work for both Java and Kotlin. For Kotlin remember we talked about two different ways to emit the constants — check how we handled the shape-selection in that output formatting module from the previous sprint, same idea applies here.

Also important: test-only constants should NOT bleed into production source. Keep them separate.

One thing that came up in the infra discussion — if nothing has changed between builds, we shouldn't be regenerating these files from scratch. The build system should recognize the outputs are still valid and pull from cache. Changing any input (including the shape/style option) must bust that cache.

If a project doesn't bother specifying a namespace/package, the tool should figure out something reasonable on its own rather than blowing up. Oh and if someone adds a second constants class alongside the primary one, both should get emitted in the same run.

Let me know if anything is unclear, I'll try to dig up the old notes.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When no explicit namespace is provided, the namespace must be derived from the project_name by replacing any character that is not a valid Java/Kotlin identifier character (e.g. hyphens, dots, spaces) with underscores. For example, 'test-project' becomes 'test_project'. This sanitized value is used both as the package declaration in the source and as the directory path segment under the output root.

When source_set is 'test', the generation task name must be 'generateTestBuildConfig' (not 'generateBuildConfig'), the output path must use 'test' instead of 'main' as the source-group segment (e.g. build/generated/sources/buildConfig/test/...), and the generated class name must be prefixed with 'Test' (e.g. 'TestBuildConfig'). Only the fields listed under test_fields are emitted in this file; the production fields are ignored for this task invocation.

Kotlin output supports two styles. The default style wraps all constants inside 'internal object BuildConfig { ... }'. When output_style is set to 'top_level_constants', constants are emitted at file scope with no enclosing object — just package declaration, imports, and bare 'internal const val' declarations. The file name and path remain BuildConfig.kt in both cases.

The generation task must be cache-aware. When the same inputs are presented on a second clean build, the task outcome must be FROM_CACHE rather than SUCCESS. If any input changes — including the output_style option — the cached result must be invalidated and the task must re-execute with outcome SUCCESS. The cache check mode emits lines like first_build_status, first_generate_outcome, second_build_status, second_generate_outcome (and optionally third_*) to stdout.

When extra_types is provided, each entry produces an additional generated source file alongside the primary BuildConfig file. Each extra type has its own type_name and its own fields list. Both files are emitted under the same namespace/package directory in a single task run. The stdout output lists each file separately with its own '--- source ---' / '--- end source ---' block, primary file first, then extra types in declaration order.

Long values that look like epoch millisecond timestamps (large numeric literals ending in 'L') must not be written with the literal value from the input if that value appears to be in the past. Instead, the generator substitutes the current build time (System.currentTimeMillis()) so the value is never a future epoch. In test output normalization the placeholder '<not_future_epoch_ms>L' is used to assert the emitted value is a non-future epoch rather than checking an exact wall-clock value.

For Java output, 'import java.lang.String;' must be emitted when any field of type String is present. Primitive types (int, long, boolean, etc.) do not generate import statements. For Kotlin output, each type that appears in the field list generates a corresponding 'import kotlin.<Type>' statement (e.g. 'import kotlin.String', 'import kotlin.Boolean'). Imports are sorted alphabetically and appear before the class/object declaration.

The generated file path follows the pattern: build/generated/sources/buildConfig/<sourceSet>/<namespace-as-path>/<ClassName>.<ext> where sourceSet is 'main' or 'test', namespace dots and underscores are used as-is to form directory segments (e.g. 'gs.test' → 'gs/test'), ClassName is BuildConfig (or TestBuildConfig for test source set, or the custom type_name for extra types), and ext is 'java' or 'kt'.

Constants must be emitted in the generated source in exactly the same order they appear in the input fields array. No alphabetical sorting or other reordering may be applied. This applies to both primary and extra-type field lists.

Java generated classes must be declared 'public final class' and must include a private no-arg constructor 'private BuildConfig() {}' (or the appropriate class name) as the last member, to prevent instantiation. Each field declaration must be followed by a blank line, including after the last field and before the private constructor block.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: check how we handled the shape-selection in that output formatting module from the previous sprint
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The output_style field in the input config selects between 'object_container' (default) and 'top_level_constants' Kotlin emission styles, implemented in the Kotlin source generator / code-writer component responsible for rendering the constants file structure.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the tool should figure out something reasonable on its own rather than blowing up
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The namespace derivation logic in the configuration resolution layer: replace non-identifier characters in project_name with underscores to produce the default package/namespace string used in both the file path and the package declaration.

The style-selection mechanism mirrors the output_style enum/string flag pattern: a string key ('top_level_constants') maps to a distinct rendering strategy, and changing that key must invalidate the build cache (treated as a cacheable task input).

The cache_check execution mode in the adapter layer, which runs the generation task twice (or three times for the Kotlin option-change scenario) and captures task outcome strings ('SUCCESS' vs 'FROM_CACHE') emitted per build invocation.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect right now — could you check the existing test cases under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ or grep the source for related patterns? The expected_output fields in those JSON files are usually the most reliable reference for exact formatting rules.

feature1_main_java_constants.json.description: Generating primary Java constants creates a compile-ready source file containing all configured primitive and text constants in declaration order.

feature2_default_namespace.json.description: When no namespace is explicitly provided, the generated source is placed under a sanitized namespace derived from the project identity.

feature3_additional_constants_type.json.description: A secondary constants type can be generated beside the primary type and contains only the constants configured for that secondary output.

feature4_test_source_set_constants.json.description: Constants configured for the test source group are generated in the test output location with the test-specific type name and values.

feature5_kotlin_object_constants.json.description: Kotlin output generates an object-style constants source with const properties for supported scalar values.

cases.expected_output:
build_status=success
task=generateBuildConfig
file=build/generated/sources/buildConfig/main/gs/test/BuildConfig.kt
--- source ---
package gs.test

import kotlin.Boolean
import kotlin.String

internal object BuildConfig {
  internal const val APP_NAME: String = "test-project"

  internal const val FEATURE_ENABLED: Boolean = true
}
--- end source ---

feature6_kotlin_top_level_constants.json.description: Kotlin output can be configured to emit constants at file scope instead of wrapping them in an object container.

cases.expected_output:
build_status=success
task=generateBuildConfig
file=build/generated/sources/buildConfig/main/gs/test/BuildConfig.kt
--- source ---
package gs.test

import kotlin.Boolean
import kotlin.String

internal const val APP_NAME: String = "test-project"

internal const val FEATURE_ENABLED: Boolean = true

feature7_field_ordering.json.description: Constants appear in the generated source in the same order they are declared by the user.

feature8_java_cacheability.json.description: A Java constants generation task is reusable from the build cache when the inputs are unchanged across clean builds.

feature9_kotlin_cacheability_and_option_.description: A Kotlin constants generation task is reusable from cache for unchanged inputs, and changing the output shape invalidates the cached result.

cases.expected_output:
first_build_status=success
first_generate_outcome=SUCCESS
second_build_status=success
second_generate_outcome=FROM_CACHE

third_generate_outcome=SUCCESS
